1.2: Of mortal men down to the very last

1.43: Spontaneous flowing answered he in turn;
1.44: For God had care for all things. For the mind
1.45: They darkened not with passion, nor concealed

1.114: Finding inventions by their lack of means.

1.121: And others were in every other art

1.130: And after these a third strong-minded race
1.131: Appeared, a race of overbearing men
1.132: And terrible, who wrought among themselves

1.135: Those men possessed of overweening heart.
1.136: And from these afterward another race
1.138: Blood-stained, perverse in counsel; of men these

1.141: For maddening wrath and sore impiety
1.142: Were sent upon them. And wars, homicides,

1.149: And later yet another race much worse

1.153: Giants perverse, foul language pouring out.
1.154: Single among all men, most just and true,
1.155: Was the most faithful Noah, full of care

1.157: From heaven thus spoke: "Noah, be of good cheer

1.159: Repentance, so that they may all be saved.

1.161: The whole race I will utterly destroy
1.162: With mighty floods of waters. Quickly now
1.163: An undecaying house I bid thee frame
1.164: Of planks strong and impervious to the wet.
1.165: I will put understanding in thy heart,
1.166: And subtile skill, and rule of measurement
1.167: And order; and for all things will I care
1.168: That thou be saved, and all who dwell with thee.
1.169: And I am He who is, and in thy heart
1.170: Do thou discern. I clothe me with the heaven,
1.171: And cast the sea around me, and for me
1.172: Earth is a footstool, and the air is poured
1.173: Around my body; and on every side
1.174: Around me runs the chorus of the stars.
1.175: Nine letters have I; of four syllables
1.176: I am discern me. The first three have each
1.177: Two letters, the remaining one the rest,
1.178: And five are mutes; and of the entire sum
1.179: The hundreds are twice eight and thrice three tens
1.180: Along with seven. Now, knowing who I am,
1.181: Be thou not uninitiate in my lore."
1.182: Thus he spoke; and great trembling seized on him
1.183: At what he heard. And then, within his mind
1.184: Having contrived each matter, he besought
1.185: The people and began with words like these:
1.186: "O men insatiate, smit with madness great,
1.187: Whatever things ye practiced they shall not
1.188: Escape God's notice; for he knows all things,
1.190: Who bade me warn you, that ye perish not.
1.191: Be sober, cut off badness, do not fight
1.192: Perforce each other with blood-guilty heart,
1.193: Nor irrigate much land with human gore.
1.194: Revere, O mortals, the supremely great
1.196: Imperishable, whose dwelling is the sky;
1.197: And do ye all entreat him—he is kind—
1.198: For life of cities and of all the world,
1.199: And of four-footed beasts and flying fowls;
1.200: Entreat him to be gracious unto all.
1.201: For when the whole unbounded world of men
1.202: Shall be destroyed by waters loud ye'll raise
1.203: A fearful cry. And suddenly for you

1.205: The fury of the mighty God shall come
1.206: Upon you. And it certainly shall be

1.208: Will send wrath if ye do not placate God
1.209: And from this time repent; and nothing more
1.210: Fretful and evil lawlessly shall ye
1.211: One to another do, but let there be
1.212: A guarding of one's self by holy life."
1.213: But when they heard him each turned up his nose,

1.216: "O men exceeding wretched, base in heart,

2.65: A life of probity. Be satisfied
2.66: With what thou hast and keep thyself from that
2.67: Which is another's. Speak not what is false,
2.68: But have a care for all things that are true.
2.69: Revere not idols vainly; but the God
2.71: And next thy parents. Render all things due,
2.72: And into unjust judgment come thou not.
2.73: Do not cast out the poor unrighteously,
2.74: Nor judge by outward show; if wickedly
2.75: Thou judgest, God hereafter will judge thee.
2.76: Avoid false testimony; tell the truth.
2.77: Maintain thy virgin purity, and guard
2.78: Love among all. Deal measures that are just;
2.79: For beautiful is measure full to all.
2.80: Strike not the scales oneside, but draw them equal.
2.82: God hates the perjured man in that he swore.
2.83: A gift proceeding out of unjust deeds
2.84: Never receive in hand. Do not steal seed;
2.86: Who took it unto scattering of life.
2.87: Indulge not vile lusts, slander not, nor kill.
2.88: Give the toil worn his hire; do not afflict
2.89: The poor man. Unto orphans help afford.
2.90: And to widows and the needy. Talk with sense;
2.91: Hold fast in heart a secret. Be unwilling
2.92: To act unjustly nor yet tolerate
2.93: Unrighteous men. Give to the poor at once
2.94: And say not, "Come to-morrow." Of thy grain
2.95: Give to the needy with perspiring hand.
2.96: He who gives alms knows how to lend to God.
2.97: Mercy redeems from death when judgment comes.
2.98: Not sacrifice, but mercy God desires
2.99: Rather than sacrifice. The naked clothe,
2.100: Share thy bread with the hungry, in thy house
2.101: Receive the shelterless and lead the blind.
2.102: Pity the shipwrecked; for the voyage is
2.103: Uncertain. To the fallen give a hand;
2.104: And save the man that stands without defense.
2.105: Common to all is suffering, life's a wheel,
2.106: Riches unstable. Having wealth, reach out
2.107: To the poor thy hand. Of what God gave to thee
2.108: Bestow thou also on the needy one.
2.109: Common is the whole life of mortal men;
2.110: But it comes out unequal. When thou seest
2.111: A poor man never banter him with words,
2.112: Nor harshly accost a man who may be blamed.
2.113: One's life in death is proven; if one did.
2.114: The unlawful or just, it shall be decided
2.115: When he to judgment comes. Disable not
2.116: Thy mind with wine nor drink excessively.
2.117: Eat not blood, and abstain from things
2.118: Offered to idols. Gird not on the sword
2.119: For slaughter, but defense; and would thou might
2.120: It neither lawlessly nor justly use:
2.121: For if thou kill an enemy thy band
2.122: Thou dost defile. Keep from thy neighbor's field,
2.123: Nor trespass on it; just is every landmark,
2.124: And trespass painful. Useful is possession
2.125: Of lawful wealth, but of unrighteous gains
2.126: 'Tis worthless. Harm not any growing fruit
2.127: Of the field. And let strangers be esteemed
2.128: In equal honor with the citizens;
2.130: Shall all experience as each other's guests;
2.131: But let there not be anyone a stranger
2.132: Among you, since, ye mortals, all of you
2.133: Are of one blood, and no land has for men
2.134: Any sure place. Wish not nor pray for wealth;
2.135: But pray to live from few things and possess
2.136: Nothing at all unjust. The love of gain
2.137: Is mother of all evil. Do not long
2.138: For gold or silver; in them there will be
2.140: A snare to men continually are gold.
2.141: And silver. Gold, of evils source, of life
2.142: Destructive, troubling all things, would that thou
2.143: Wert not to mortals such a longed-for bane!
2.144: For wars, because of thee, and pillaging
2.145: And murders come, and children hate their sires,
2.146: And brothers and sisters those of their own blood.
2.147: Plot no deceit, and do not arm thy heart
2.148: Against a friend. Keep not concealed within
2.149: A different thought from what thou speakest forth;
2.150: Nor, like rock-clinging polyp, change with place.
2.151: But with all be frank, and things from the soul
2.153: Commits a wrong, an evil man is he;
2.154: But he that does it under force, the end
2.155: I tell not; but lot each man's will be right.
2.156: Pride not thyself in wisdom, power, or wealth;
2.157: God only is the wise and mighty one
2.158: And full of riches. Do not vex thy heart
2.159: With evils that are past; for what is done
2.160: Can never be undone. Let not thy hand
2.161: Be hasty, but ferocious passion curb;
2.162: For many times has one in striking done
2.164: Be common, neither great nor overmuch.
2.165: Excessive good has not brought forth to men
2.166: That which is helpful. And much luxury
2.167: Leads to immoderate lusts. Much wealth is proud,
2.168: And makes one grow to wanton violence.
2.170: Destructive madness. Anger is a lust,
2.171: And when it is excessive it is wrath.
2.172: The zeal of good men is a noble thing,
2.173: But of the base is base. Of wicked men
2.174: The boldness is destructive, but renown
2.175: Follows that of the good. To be revered
2.176: Is virtuous love, but that of Cypris works
2.177: Increase of shame. A silly man is called
2.179: With moderation eat, drink, and converse;
2.180: Of all things moderation is the best;
2.181: But trespass of its limit brings to grief.
2.182: Be not thou envious, faithless, or abusive,
2.183: Or evil-minded, or a false deceiver.
2.184: Be prudent and abstain from shameless deeds.
2.185: Imitate not what's evil, but leave thou
2.186: Vengeance to justice; for persuasion is
2.187: A useful thing, but strife engenders strife.
2.188: Trust not too quickly ere thou see the end.
2.189: This is the contest, these are the rewards;
2.190: These are the prizes; this the gate of life
2.192: Which God in heaven unto most righteous men
2.194: And through this gate shall gloriously pass
2.195: Those who shall then receive the victor's crown.
2.196: But when this sign shall everywhere appear—
2.197: Children with gray hair on their temples born—
2.198: And human sufferings, famines, plagues, and wars,
2.199: And change of times, and many a tearful wail,
2.200: Ah! of how many parents in the lands
2.201: Will children mourn and piteously weep,
2.202: And with shrouds bury flesh and limbs in earth,
2.203: Mother of peoples, with the blood and dust
2.204: Themselves defiling. O ye wretched men
2.205: Of the last generation, evil doers,
2.207: That when the tribes of women do not bear
2.208: The harvest time of mortal men is come.
2.209: Near is the ruin when impostors come
2.210: Instead of prophets speaking on the earth.
2.211: And Beliar shall come and many signs
2.212: Perform for men. And then of holy men,
2.213: Elect and faithful, there shall be confusion,
2.214: And pillaging of them and of the Hebrews.
2.215: And there shall be upon them fearful wrath
2.216: When from the east a people of twelve tribes
2.217: Shall come in search of kindred Hebrew people
2.218: Whom Assyrian shoot destroyed; and over these
2.219: Shall nations perish. But they afterwards
2.220: Shall over men exceeding mighty rule,
2.221: Elect and faithful Hebrews, and enslave
2.222: Them as before, since their power ne'er shall fail,
2.223: He that is highest of all, the all-surveying,
2.224: Dwelling in heaven, will scatter sleep on men,
2.225: Covering the eyelids o'er. O blessed servants
2.226: Whom when the Master comes he finds awake!
2.227: And they all watch at all times and expect
2.228: With sleepless eyes. For it will be at dawn
2.229: Or eve or midday; but he sure shall come,
2.230: And it shall be as I say, it shall be,
2.231: To them that sleep, that from the starry heaven
2.232: The stars at midday will to all appear
2.233: With the two lights as the time hastens on.
2.234: And then the Tishbite, urging from the heaven
2.235: His chariot celestial, and on earth
2.236: Arriving, shall to all the world display
2.237: Three evil signs of life to be destroyed.
2.238: Alas for all the women in that day
2.239: Who shall be found with burden in the womb!
2.240: Alas for all who suckle tender babes!
2.241: Alas for all who shall dwell on the waves!
2.242: Alas for women who shall see that day!
2.243: For a dark mist shall hide the boundless world,
2.244: East, west, and south, and north. And then shall flow
2.245: A mighty stream of burning fire from heaven.
2.246: And every place consume, earth, ocean vast,
2.247: And gleaming sea, and lakes and rivers, springs,
2.248: And cruel Hades and the heavenly sky.
2.249: And heavenly lights shall break up into one
2.251: For stars from heaven shall fall into all seas.
2.252: And all the souls of men shall gnash their teeth
2.253: Burned both by sulphur stream and force of fire
2.254: In ravenous soil, and ashes hide all things.
2.255: And then of the world all the elements
2.256: Shall be bereft, air, earth, sea, light, sky, days,
2.257: Nights; and no longer in the air shall fly
2.258: Birds without number, nor shall living things
2.259: That swim the sea swim any more at all,
2.260: Nor freighted vessel o'er the billows pass,
2.261: Nor kine straight-guiding plow the field, nor sound
2.262: Of furious winds; but he shall fuse all things
2.263: Together, and shall pick out what is pure.
2.264: But when the immortal God's eternal angels
2.266: And Azael, they that know how many evils
2.267: Anyone did before, shall from dark gloom.
2.268: Then lead to judgment all the souls of men
2.269: Before the judgment-seat of the great God
2.271: One only, himself the almighty One,
2.272: Who shall be judge of mortals; and to them
2.273: That dwell beneath will then the heavenly One
2.274: Give souls and spirit and voice, and also bones
2.275: Fitted with joints unto all kinds of flesh,
2.276: And both the flesh and sinews, veins and skin
2.277: About the body, and hair as before;
2.278: Divinely fashioned and with breathing moved
2.279: Shall bodies of those on earth one day be raised.
2.280: And then shall Uriel, mighty angel, break
2.281: The bolts of stern and lasting adamant
2.282: Which, monstrous, hold the brazen gates of Hades,
2.283: Straight cast them down, and unto judgment lead
2.284: All forms that have endured much suffering,
2.285: Chiefly the shapes of Titans born of old,
2.286: And giants, and all whom the deluge whelmed,
2.287: And all that perished in the billowy seas,
2.288: And all that furnished banquet for the beasts
2.289: And creeping things and fowls, these in a mass
2.291: And also those whom flesh-devouring fire
2.292: Destroyed in flame, even these shall he collect
2.293: And place before the judgment-seat of God,
2.294: And when the high-thundering Lord of Sabaoth
2.295: Making an end of fate shall raise the dead,
2.296: Sit on his heavenly throne, and firmly fix
2.297: The mighty pillar, then amid the clouds
2.300: In glory with pure angels, and shall sit
2.301: At the right hand on the great judgment-seat
2.302: To judge the life of pious and the way
2.303: Of impious men. And Moses, the great friend
2.304: Of the Most High, shall come enrobed in flesh;
2.305: Also great Abraham himself shall come,
2.308: Those whom the Hebrews slow. But he'll destroy
2.310: Who are to be judged at the judgment-seat,
2.311: That worthy recompense they may receive
2.312: And pay for all each did in mortal life.
2.313: And then shall all pass through the burning stream
2.314: Of flame unquenchable; but all the just
2.315: Shall be saved; and the godless furthermore
2.316: Shall to all ages perish, all who did
2.318: And all who are accomplices therein,
2.319: Liars and thieves, and ruiners of home,
2.321: And marriage-breakers pouring forth vile words,
2.323: And all who left the great immortal God,
2.324: Became blasphemers, did the pious harm,
2.325: Destroying faith and killing righteous men;
2.326: And all that with a shamelessness deceitful
2.327: And double-faced rush in as presbyters
2.329: Give unjust judgments, yielding to false words..
2.330: More hurtful than the leopards and the wolves
2.331: And more vile; and all that are grossly proud;
2.332: And usurers, who gains on gains amass
2.333: And damage orphans and widows in each thing;
2.334: And all that give to widows and to orphans
2.335: The fruit of unjust deeds, and all that cast
2.336: Reproach in giving from their own hard toils;
2.337: And all that left their parents in old age,
2.338: Not paying them at all, nor offering
2.339: To parents filial duty, and all who
2.340: Were disobedient and against their sires
2.341: Spoke a harsh word; and all that pledges took
2.342: And then denied them; and the servants all
2.343: Who were against their masters, and again
2.344: Those who licentiously defiled the flesh;
2.345: And all who loosed the girdle of the maid
2.346: For secret intercourse, and all who caused
2.347: Abortions, and all who their offspring cast
2.349: And sorceresses with them, and these wrath
2.350: Of the heavenly and immortal God shall drive
2.351: Against a pillar, where shall all around.
2.352: In a circle flow a restless stream of fire;
2.353: And deathless angels of the immortal God,
2.354: Who ever is, shall bind with lasting bonds
2.355: In chains of flaming fire and from above
2.356: Punish them all by scourge most terribly;
2.357: And in Gehenna, in the gloom of night,
2.358: Shall they be cast 'neath many horrid beasts
2.359: Of Tartarus, where darkness is immense.
2.360: But when there shall be many punishments
2.361: Enforced on all who had an evil heart,
2.362: Yet afterward shall there a fiery wheel
2.363: From a great river circle them around,
2.364: Because they had a care for wicked deeds.
2.365: And then one here, another there, shall sires,
2.366: Young children, mothers, nursing babes, in tears
2.367: Wail their most piteous fate. No fill of tears
2.368: Shall be for them, nor piteous voice be heard
2.369: Of them that moan, one here, another there,
2.370: But long worn under dark, dank Tartarus
2.371: Aloud shall they cry; and they shall repay
2.372: In cursed places thrice as much as all
2.373: The evil work they did, burned with much fire;
2.374: And all of them, consumed by raging thirst
2.375: And hunger, shall in anguish gnash their teeth
2.376: And call death beautiful, and death shall flee
2.377: Away from them. For neither death nor night
2.378: Shall ever give them rest. And many things in vain
2.379: Will they ask of the God that rules on high,
2.380: And then will he his face turn openly
2.381: Away from them. For he to erring men
2.382: Gave, in seven ages for repentance, signs
2.383: By the hands of a virgin undefiled.
2.384: But the others, all to whom right and fair works
2.385: And piety and thoughts most just were dear,
2.386: Shall angels, bearing through the burning stream,
2.387: Lead unto light and life exempt from care,
2.388: Where comes the immortal way of the great God
2.389: And fountains thrce—of honey, wine, and milk.
2.390: And equal land for all, divided not
2.391: By walls or fences, more abundant fruits
2.392: Spontaneous shall then bear, and the course
2.393: Of life be common and wealth unapportioned,
2.394: For there no longer will be poor nor rich,
2.395: Tyrant nor slave, nor any great nor small,
2.396: Nor kings nor leaders; all alike in common.
2.397: No more at all will one say, "night has come,"
2.399: Nor shall there many days of anxious care,
2.400: Nor spring, nor winter, nor the summer-heat,
2.401: Nor autumn he nor marriage, nor yet death,
2.402: Nor sales, nor purchases, nor set of sun
2.403: Nor rising; for a long day will God make.
2.404: And to the pious will the almighty God
2.406: When they shall ask the imperishable God:
2.407: That he will suffer men from raging fire
2.408: And endless gnawing anguish to be saved;
2.409: And this will he do. For hereafter he
2.410: Will pluck them from the restless flame, elsewhere
2.411: Remove them, and for his own people's sake
2.412: Send them to other and eternal life
2.413: With the immortals, in Elysian field,
2.414: Where move far-stretching billows of the lake
2.416: Ah, miserable woman that I am!
2.417: What shall I be in that day? for I sinned—
2.418: Being busy foolishly about all things,
2.419: Caring for neither marriage-bond nor reason;
2.420: But even in my wealthy husband's house
2.421: I shut the needy out; and formerly
2.423: But, Saviour, though I shameless things performed,
2.424: Do thou from my tormentors rescue me,
2.425: A shameless woman. And I pray thee now
2.426: Make me to rest a little from my song,
2.427: Holy Giver of manna, King of the great realm.
3.1: O thou high-thundering blessed heavenly One,
3.2: Who hast set in their place the cherubim,
3.3: I, who have uttered what is all too true,
3.4: Entreat thee, let me have a little rest;
3.5: For my heart has grown weary from within.
3.6: But why again leaps my heart, and my soul
3.7: With a whip smitten from within constrained
3.8: To utter forth its message unto all?
3.9: But yet again will I proclaim all things
3.10: Which God commands me to proclaim to men,
3.11: O men, that in your image have a form
3.12: Fashioned of God, why do ye vainly stray
3.13: And walk not in the straight way, always mindful
3.14: Of the immortal Maker? God is one,
3.18: Him sculptor's hand made not, nor is his form
3.19: Shown by man's art from gold or ivory;
3.20: But he, eternal Lord, proclaims himself
3.21: As one who is and was erst and shall be
3.22: Again hereafter. For who being mortal
3.23: Can see God with his eyes? Or who shall bear
3.24: To hear the only name of heaven's great God,
3.25: The ruler of the world? He by his word
3.26: Created all things, even heaven and sea,
3.27: And tireless sun, and full moon and bright stars,
3.28: And mighty mother Tethys, springs and rivers,
3.29: Imperishable fire, and days and nights.
3.30: This is the God who formed four-lettered Adam,
3.31: The first one formed, and filling with his name
3.32: East, west, and south, and north. The same is he
3.33: Who fixed the pattern of the human form,
3.34: And made wild beasts, and creeping things, and fowls.
3.35: Ye do not worship neither fear ye God,
3.36: But vainly go astray and bow the knee
3.37: To serpents, and make offering to cats,
3.38: And idols, and stone images of men,
3.39: And sit before the doors of godless temples;
3.40: Ye guard him who is God, who keeps all things,
3.41: And merry with the wickedness of stones
3.42: Forget the judgment of the immortal Saviour
3.43: Who made the heaven and earth. Alas! a race
3.44: That has delight in blood, deceitful, vile,
3.45: Ungodly, of false, double-tongued, immoral men,
3.47: An evil madness raving in their hearts,
3.48: For themselves plundering, having shameless soul;
3.49: For no one who has riches will impart
3.50: To another, but dire wickedness shall be
3.51: Among all mortals, and for sake of gain
3.52: Will many widows not at all keep faith,
3.53: But secretly love others, and the bond
3.54: Of life those who have husbands do not keep.
3.55: But when Rome shall o'er Egypt also rule
3.56: Governing always, then shall there appear
3.57: The greatest kingdom of the immortal King
3.58: Over men. And a holy Lord shall come
3.59: To hold the scepter over every land
3.60: Unto all ages of fast-hastening time.
3.61: And then shall come inexorable wrath
3.62: On Latin men; three shall by piteous fate
3.63: Endamage Rome. And perish shall all men,
3.64: With their own houses, when from heaven shall flow
3.65: A fiery cataract. Ah, wretched me!
3.66: When shall that day and when shall judgment come
3.67: Of the immortal God, the mighty King?
3.68: But just now, O ye cities, ye are built
3.69: And all adorned with temples and race-grounds,
3.70: Markets, and images of wood, of gold,
3.71: Of silver and of stone, that ye may come
3.72: Unto the bitter day. For it shall come,
3.73: When there shall pass among all men a stench
3.74: Of brimstone. Yet each thing will I declare,
3.75: In all the cities where men suffer ills.
3.77: From the Sebastenes Beliar shall come
3.78: Hereafter, and the height of hills shall he
3.79: Establish, and shall make the sea stand still
3.80: And the great fiery sun and the bright moon;
3.81: Work before men: but nothing shall be brought
3.82: By him unto completion but deceit,
3.83: And many mortals shall he lead astray—
3.84: Hebrews both true and choice, and lawless men
3.85: Besides who never gave ear to God's word.
3.86: But when the threatenings of the mighty God
3.87: Shall draw near, and a flaming power shall come
3.88: By billow to the earth, it shall consume
3.89: Both Beliar and all the haughty men
3.90: Who put their trust in him. And thereupon
3.91: Shall the whole world be governed by the hands
3.92: Of a woman and obedient everywhere.
3.93: Then when a widow shall o'er all the world
3.94: Gain the rule, and east in the mighty sea
3.95: Both gold and silver, also brass and iron
3.96: Of short-lived men into the deep shall east,
3.97: Then all the elements shall be bereft
3.98: Of order, when the God who dwells on high
3.99: Shall roll the heaven, even as a scroll is rolled;
3.100: And to the mighty earth and sea shall fall
3.101: The entire multiform sky; and there shall flow
3.102: A tireless cataract of raging fire,
3.103: And it shall burn the land, and burn the sea,
3.104: And heavenly sky, and night, and day, and melt
3.105: Creation itself together and pick out
3.106: What is pure. No more laughing spheres of light,
3.107: Nor night, nor dawn, nor many days of care,
3.108: Nor spring, nor winter, nor the summer-time,
3.109: Nor autumn. And then of the mighty God
3.110: The judgment midway in a mighty age
3.111: Shall come, when all these things shall come to pass.
3.113: O navigable waters and each land
3.114: Of the Orient and of the Occident,
3.115: Subject shall all things be to him who comes
3.116: Himself became first conscious of his power.
3.118: But when the threatenings of the mighty God
3.119: Are fulfilled, which he threatened mortals once,
3.120: When in Assyrian land they built a tower;—
3.121: To mount aloft into the starry heaven;
3.122: But on the air the Immortal straightway put
3.123: A mighty force; and then winds from above
3.124: Cast down the great tower and stirred mortals up
3.125: To wrangling with each other; therefore men
3.126: Gave to that city the name of Babylon);—
3.127: Now when the tower fell and the tongues of men
3.128: Turned to all sorts of sounds, straightway all earth
3.129: Was filled with men and kingdoms were divided;
3.130: And then the generation tenth appeared
3.131: Of mortal men, from the time when the flood
3.132: Came upon earlier men. And Cronos reigned,
3.133: And Titan and Iapetus; and men called them
3.134: Best offspring of Gaia and of Uranus,
3.135: Giving to them names both of earth and heaven,
3.136: Since they were very first of mortal men.
3.137: So there were three divisions of the earth
3.138: According to the allotment of each man,
3.139: And each one having his own portion reigned
3.140: And fought not; for a father's oaths were there
3.141: And equal were their portions. But the time
3.142: Complete of old age on the father came,
3.143: And he died; and the sons infringing oaths
3.144: Stirred up against each other bitter strife,
3.145: Which one should have the royal rank and rule
3.146: Over all mortals; and against each other
3.147: Cronos and Titan fought. But Rhea and Gaia,
3.148: And Aphrodite fond of crowns, Demeter,
3.149: And Hestia and Dione of fair locks
3.150: Brought them to friendship, and together called
3.151: All who were kings, both brothers and near kin,
3.152: And others of the same ancestral blood,
3.153: And they judged Cronos should reign king of all,
3.154: For he was oldest and of noblest form.
3.155: But Titan laid on Cronos mighty oaths
3.156: To rear no male posterity, that he
3.157: Himself might reign when age and fate should come
3.158: To Cronos. And whenever Rhea bore
3.159: Beside her sat the Titans, and all males
3.160: In pieces tore, but let the females live
3.161: To be reared by the mother. But when now
3.162: At the third birth the august Rhea bore,
3.163: She brought forth Hera first; and when they saw.
3.164: A female offspring, the fierce Titan men
3.165: Betook them to their homes. And thereupon
3.166: Rhea a male child bore, and having bound
3.167: Three men of Crete by oath she quickly sent
3.168: Him into Phrygia to be reared apart
3.169: In secret; therefore did they name him Zeus,
3.170: For be was sent away. And thus she sent
3.172: And Pluto, third, did Rhea yet again,
3.173: Noblest of women, at Dodona bear,
3.174: Whence flows Europus' river's liquid course,
3.175: And with Pencus mixed pours in the sea
3.176: Its water, and men call it Stygian.
3.177: But when the Titans heard that there were sons
3.178: Kept secretly, whom Cronos and his wife
3.179: Rhea begat, then Titan sixty youths
3.180: Together gathered, and held fast in chains
3.181: Cronos and his wife Rhea, and concealed
3.182: Them in the earth and guarded them in bonds,
3.183: And then the sons of powerful Cronos heard,

3.732: On the great altar. And in righteousness,

3.734: Blest shall they dwell in cities and rich fields.
3.735: And prophets shall be set on high for them
3.736: By the Immortal, bringing great delight
3.737: Unto all mortals. For to them alone
3.738: The mighty God his gracious counsel gave
3.739: And faith and noblest thought within their hearts;
3.740: They have not by vain things been led astray,
3.741: Nor pay they honor to the works of men
3.742: Made of gold, brass, silver, and ivory,
3.743: Nor statues of dead gods of wood and stone
3.744: Besmeared clay, figures of the painter's art,
3.745: And all that empty-minded mortals will;
3.746: But they lift up their pure arms unto heaven,
3.747: Rise from the couch at daybreak, always hands
3.748: With water cleanse, and honor only Him
3.749: Who is immortal and who ever rules,
3.750: And then their parents; and above all men
3.751: Do they respect the lawful marriage-bed;
3.752: And they have not base intercourse with boys,
3.753: As do Phoenicians, Latins, and Egyptians
3.754: And spacious Greece, and nations many more
3.755: Of Persians and Galatians and all Asia,
3.756: Transgressing the immortal God's pure law
3.757: Which they were under. Therefore on all men
3.758: Will the Immortal put bane, famine, pains,
3.759: Groans, war, and pestilence and mournful woes;
3.760: Because they would not honor piously
3.761: The immortal Sire of all men, but revered
3.762: And worshiped idols made with hands, which things
3.763: Mortals themselves will cast down and for shame
3.764: Conceal in clefts of rocks, when a young king,
3.765: The seventh of Egypt, shall rule his own land,
3.766: Reckoned from the dominion of the Greeks,
3.767: Which countless Macedonian men shall rule;
3.768: And there shall come from Asia a great king,
3.769: A fiery eagle, who with foot and horse
3.770: Shall cover all the land, cut up all things,
3.771: And fill all things with evils; he will cast
3.772: The Egyptian kingdom down; and taking off
3.773: All its possessions carry them away
3.774: Over the spacious surface of the sea.
3.775: And then shall they before the mighty God,
3.776: The King immortal, bend the fair white knee
3.777: On the much-nourishing earth; and all the works
3.778: Made with hands shall fall by a flame of fire.
3.779: And then will God bestow great joy on men;
3.780: For land and trees and countless flocks of sheep
3.781: Their genuine fruit to men shall offer-wine,
3.782: And the sweet honey, and white milk, and wheat,
3.783: Which is for mortals of all things the best.
3.784: But thou, O mortal full of various wiles,
3.785: Do not delay and loiter, but do thou,
3.786: Tossed to and fro, turn and propitiate God.
3.787: Offer to God your hecatombs of bulls
3.788: And firstling lambs and goats, as times revolve.
3.789: But him propitiate, the immortal God,
3.790: If haply he show mercy. For he is
3.791: The only God, and other there is none,
3.792: And honor justice and oppress no man,
3.793: For these things the Immortal doth enjoin
3.794: On miserable men. But do thon heed
3.795: The cause of the wrath of the mighty God,
3.796: When on all mortals there shall come the height
3.797: Of pestilence and conquered they shall meet.
3.798: A fearful judgment, and king shall seize king
3.799: And wrest his land away, and nations bring
3.800: Ruin on nations and lords plunder tribes,
3.801: And chiefs all flee into another land,
3.802: And the land change its men, and foreign rule
3.803: Ravage all Hellas and drain the rich land
3.804: Of its wealth, and to strife among themselves
3.805: Because of gold and silver they shall come—
3.806: The love of gain an evil shepherdess
3.807: Will be for cities—in a foreign land.
3.808: And they shall all be without burial,
3.809: And vultures and wild beasts of earth shall spoil
3.810: Their flesh; and when these things are brought to pass,
3.811: Vast earth shall waste the relics of the dead.
3.812: And all unsown shall it be and unplowed,
3.813: Proclaiming sad the filth of men defiled.
3.814: Many lengths of time in the revolving years,
3.815: And shields and javelins and all sorts of arms;
3.816: Nor shall the forest wood be cut for fire.
3.817: And then shall God send from the East a king,
3.818: Who shall make all earth cease from evil war,
3.819: Killing some, others binding with strong oaths.
3.820: And he will not by his own counsels do
3.821: All these things, but obey the good decrees
3.822: Of God the mighty. And with goodly wealth,
3.823: With gold and silver and purple ornament,
3.824: The temple of the mighty God again
3.825: Shall be weighed down; and the full-bearing earth
3.826: And the sea shall be filled full of good things.
3.827: And kings against each other shall begin
3.828: To hold ill will, in heart abetting evils.
3.829: Envy is not a good to wretched men.
3.830: But again kings of nations on this land
3.831: Shall rush in masses, bringing on themselves
3.832: Destruction; for they'll purpose to despoil
3.833: The great God's temple and the noblest men.
3.834: What time they reach the land, polluted kings
3.835: Shall set around the city each his throne
3.836: And have his people that obey not God.
3.837: And then shall God speak with a mighty voice
3.838: To all rude people of an empty mind,
3.839: And judgment from the mighty God shall come
3.840: Upon them, and they all shall be destroyed
3.841: By his immortal arm. And fiery swords
3.842: Shall fall from heaven on earth; and great bright lights
3.843: Shall come down flaming in the midst of men.
3.844: And in those days shall earth, all-mother, reel
3.845: By his immortal arm, and shoals of fish
3.846: In the deep sea, and all wild beasts of earth,
3.847: And countless tribes of winged fowl, and all
3.848: The souls of men and every sea shall tremble
3.849: Before the face of the Immortal One,
3.850: And there shall be dismay. Iligh mountain peaks
3.851: And monstrous hills shall he asunder break,
3.852: And to all shall dark Erebus appear.
3.853: And misty gorges in the lofty bills
3.854: Shall be full of the dead; and rocks shall stream
3.855: With blood and every torrent fill the plain.
3.856: And well-built walls of evil-minded men
3.857: Shall all fall to the earth, since they knew not
3.858: The law nor judgment of the mighty God,
3.859: But with a senseless soul all hurried on
3.860: Against the temple and raised up their spears.
3.861: And God shall judge all by war and by sword
3.862: And by fire and by overwhelming storm;
3.863: And brimstone there shall be from heaven, and stones
3.864: And great and grievous hail; and death shall come.
3.865: Upon the quadrupeds. And then shall they
3.866: Know God, the Immortal, who performs these things;
3.867: And wailing, and upon the boundless earth
3.868: Shall be at once a shout of perishing men;
3.869: And all the unholy shall be bathed in blood;
3.870: And earth herself shall also drink the blood
3.871: Of the perishing, and beasts be gorged with flesh.
3.872: And all these things the great eternal God
3.873: Himself bade me proclaim. And that shall not
3.875: Whatever only in my heart he put;
3.876: For truthful is God's spirit in the world.
3.877: But children of the mighty God shall all
3.878: Again around the temple live in peace,
3.879: Rejoicing in those things which he shall give
3.880: Who is Creator, righteous Judge and King.
3.881: For he himself, great, present far and wide,
3.882: Shall be a shelter, as on all sides round
3.883: A wall of flaming fire. And they shall be
3.884: In cities and in country without war.
3.885: For not the hand of evil war, but rather
3.886: The Immortal shall himself be their defender
3.887: And the hand of the Holy One. And then shall all
3.888: The islands and the cities tell how much
3.889: The immortal God loves those men; for all things
3.890: Help them in conflict and deliver them—
3.891: Heaven, and divinely fashioned sun, and moon.
3.892: And in those days shall carth, all-mother, reel.
3.893: Sweet word shall they send from their mouths in hymns:
3.894: "Come, falling on the earth let us all pray
3.895: The immortal King, and great eternal God.
3.896: To the temple let us in procession go,
3.897: Since he alone is Lord; and let us all
3.898: Meditate on the law of God most high,
3.899: Which is most righteous of all (laws) on earth.
3.900: And from the path of the Immortal we
3.901: Have wandered and with senseless soul we honor
3.902: Works made by hand and wooden images
3.903: Of dead men." These things souls of faithful meu
3.904: Shall cry out: "Come, having at the house of God
3.905: Fallen on our faces, let us with our hymns
3.906: Make joy to God the Father at our homes,
3.907: Supplied through all our land with arms of foes
3.908: Seven lengths of time in the revolving years;
3.909: Even shields and helmets and all sorts of arms,
3.910: And a great store of bows and arrows barbed;
3.911: For forest wood shall not be cut for fire."
3.912: But, wretched Hellas, stop thy arrogance.
3.913: And be wise; and entreat the Immortal One
3.914: Magnanimous, and be upon thy guard.
3.915: Send now against this city yet again
3.916: The people inconsiderate, who are come
3.917: Out of the holy land of the mighty One.
3.918: Do not move Camarina; for 'tis better
3.919: She be unmoved; a leopard from the lair,
3.920: Do thou not let an evil meet with thee.
3.921: But keep off, do not hold within thy breast
3.923: Ready for mighty contest. And serve God
3.924: The mighty, that thou mayest share those things;
3.925: And when that fated day shall reach its end
3.926: And judgment of the immortal God shall come
3.927: To mortals, judgment great and power shall come
3.928: Upon men. For all-mother earth shall yield
3.929: To mortals best fruit boundless, wheat, wine, oil;
3.930: Also from heaven a delightful drink
3.931: Of honey sweet, and trees shall give their fruit,
3.932: And fatted sheep and cattle there shall be,
3.933: Young lambs and kids of goats; earth shall break forth
3.934: With sweet springs of white milk; and of good things
3.935: The cities shall be full and fat the fields;
3.936: Nor sword nor uproar shall be on the earth;
3.937: No more shall earth groan heavily and quake;
3.938: Nor shall war longer be on earth, nor drought,
3.939: Nor famine, nor the fruit-destroying hail;
3.940: But great peace shall be upon all the earth,
3.941: And king to king be friend until the end
3.942: Of the age, and o'er all earth common law
3.943: Will the Immortal in the starry heaven
3.944: Perfect for men, touching whatever things
3.945: Have been by miserable mortals done;
3.946: For he alone is God, there is no other;
3.947: And the stern rage of men he'll burn with fire.
3.948: But change entirely the thoughts in thy heart,
3.949: And flee unrighteous worship; serve the One
3.951: And deeds of lewdness; thine own offspring rear
3.952: And do not murder; for the Immortal One
3.953: Is angry with him who in these things sins.
3.954: And then a kingdom over all mankind
3.955: Shall he raise up for ages, who once gave
3.956: Holy law to the pious, unto whom
3.957: He pledged to open every land, the world.
3.958: And portals of the blessed, and all joys,
3.959: And mind immortal and eternal bliss.
3.960: And out of every land unto the house
3.961: Of the great God shall they bring frankincense
3.962: And gifts, and there shall be no other house
3.963: To be inquired of by men yet to be,
3.964: But what God gave for faithful men to honor;
3.965: For mortals temple of the mighty God
3.966: Shall call it. And all pathways of the plain
3.967: And rough hills and high mountains and wild waves
3.968: Of the deep shall be easy in those days
3.969: For crossing and for sailing; for all peace
3.970: On the land of the good shall come; and sword
3.971: Shall prophets of the mighty God remove;
3.972: For they are judges and the righteous kings
3.973: Of mortals. And there shall be righteous wealth
3.974: Among mankind; for of the mighty God
3.975: This is the judgment and also the power.
3.976: Be of good cheer, O maiden, and be glad;
3.977: For he who made the heaven and earth gave thee
3.978: Joy in thy age. And he will dwell in thee;
3.979: And thine shall be immortal light; and wolves
3.980: And lambs shall in the mountains feed on grass
3.981: Together, and with kids shall leopards graze;
3.982: And bears shall lodge among the pasturing calves;
3.983: And the carnivorous lion shall eat chaff
3.984: At the manger like the cow; and little children
3.985: In bonds shall lead them; for he will make beasts
3.986: Helpless on earth. With babes shall fall asleep
3.987: Serpents, along with asps, and do no harm;
3.988: For over them shall be the hand of God.
3.989: Now tell I thee a sign exceeding clear,
3.990: That thou may'st know when the end of all things
3.991: On earth shall be. When in the starry heaven
3.992: Swords shall by night point straight toward west and cast,
3.993: Straightway shall there be also from the heaven
3.994: A cloud of dust borne forth to all the earth,
3.995: And the sun's brightness in the midst of heaven
3.996: Shall be eclipsed and the moon's beams appear
3.997: And come again on earth; by drops of blood
3.998: Distilling from the rocks a sign shall be;
3.999: And in the cloud shall ye behold a war
3.1000: Of foot and horse, like the chase of wild beasts
3.1001: In the dense fog. This end of all things God
3.1002: Shall consummate, whose dwelling is in heaven.
3.1003: But all must sacrifice to the great King.
3.1004: These things I show thee, I who madly left
3.1005: The long walls of Assyrian Babylon
3.1006: For Hellas to proclaim to all the wrath
3.1009: And that I might to mortals prophesy
3.1010: Of mysteries divine. And men shall say
3.1011: Of Erythre born, shameless; others say
3.1012: That I'm a Sibyl, born of mother Circe
3.1013: And father Gnostos, raving mad and false;
3.1014: But at that time when all things come to pass
3.1015: Ye shall remember me, and no one more
3.1016: Shall call me mad, the great God's prophetess.
3.1017: For he showed me what happened formerly
3.1018: To my ancestors; what things were the first
3.1019: Those God made known to me; and in my mind
3.1020: Did God put all things to be afterwards,
3.1021: That I might prophesy of things to come,
3.1022: And things that were, and tell them unto men,
3.1023: For when the world was deluged with a flood
3.1024: Of waters, and one man of good repute
3.1025: Alone was left and in a wooden house
3.1026: Sailed o'er the waters with the beasts and birds,
3.1027: In order that the world might be refilled,
3.1028: I was his son's bride and was of his race
3.1029: To whom the first things happened, and the last
3.1030: Were all made known; and thus from mine own mouth
3.1031: Let all these truthful things remain declared.
4.1: People of boastful Asia and of Europe,
4.2: Hear how much, all too true, I am about,
4.3: Through a mouth many-toned, from my great hall
4.4: To prophesy; no oracle am I
4.5: Of lying Phœbus, whom vain men called god,
4.6: And further falsified by calling seer;
4.7: But of the mighty God, whom hands of men
4.8: Formed not like speechless idols carved of stone.
4.9: For he has not for his abode a stone
4.10: Most dumb and toothless to a temple drawn,
4.11: Of mortals a dishonor very sore;
4.12: For he may not be seen from earth nor measured
4.13: By mortal eyes, nor formed by mortal hand;
4.14: He, looking down at once on all, is seen
4.15: Himself by no one; his are murky night,
4.16: And day, and sun, and stars, and moon, and seas
4.17: With fish, and land, and rivers, and the mouth
4.18: Of springs perennial, creatures meant for life,
4.19: And rains at once producing fruit of field
4.20: And tree and vine and oil. This God a whip
4.21: Struck through my heart within to make me tell
4.22: Truly to men what things have now befallen
4.23: And how much shall befall them yet again
4.24: From the first generation to the eleventh;
4.25: For he himself by bringing them to pass
4.26: Will prove all things. But do thou in all things,
4.27: O people, to the Sibyl give an ear,
4.28: Who pours from hallowed mouth a truthful voice.
4.29: Blessed of men shall they be on the earth
4.30: As many as shall love the mighty God,
4.31: Offering him praise before they drink and eat;
4.32: Trusting in piety. When they behold
4.33: Temples and altars, figures of dumb stones,
4.34: Stone images and statues made with hands
4.35: Polluted with the blood of living things
4.37: They will reject them all; and they will look
4.38: To the great glory of one God and not
4.40: Of stolen gain, which things most horrid are;
4.41: Nor shameful longing for another's bed
4.42: Have they, nor vile and hateful lust of males.
4.44: Shall other men, that love a shameless life,
4.45: Not ever imitate; but, mocking them
4.46: With jest and joke like babes in senselessness,
4.47: They'll falsely charge to them as many deeds
4.48: Blameful and wicked as they do themselves.
4.49: For slow is the whole race of human kind
4.50: To believe. But when judgment of the world
4.51: And mortals comes, which God himself shall bring
4.52: Judging at once the impious and the pious,
4.53: Then indeed shall he send the ungodly back
4.54: To lower darkness and then they shall know
4.55: How much impiety they wrought; but the pious
4.56: Shall still remain upon the fruitful land,
4.57: God giving to them breath and life and grace.
4.58: But these things all in the tenth generation
4.59: Shall come to pass; and now what things shall be
4.60: From the first generation, those I'll tell.
4.61: First over all mortals shall Assyrians rule,
4.62: And for six generations hold the power
4.63: Of the world, from the time the God of heaven
4.64: Being wroth against the cities and all men
4.65: Sea with a bursting deluge covered earth.
4.66: Them shall the Medes o'erpower, but on the throne
4.67: For two generations only shall exult;
4.68: In which times these events shall come to pass:
4.69: Dark night shall come at the mid hour of day;
4.70: And from the heaven the stars and circling moon
4.71: Shall disappear; and earth in tumult shaken
4.72: By a great earthquake shall throw many cities
4.73: And works of men headlong; and from the deep
4.74: Then shall peer out the islands of the sea.
4.75: But when the great Euphrates shall with blood
4.76: Be surging, then shall there be also set
4.77: Between the Medes and Persians dreadful strife
4.78: In battle; and the Medes shall fall and fly
4.79: 'Neath Persian spears beyond the mighty water
4.80: Of Tigris. And the Persian power shall be
4.81: Greatest in all the world, and they shall have
4.82: One generation of most prosperous rule.
4.83: And there shall be as many evil deeds.
4.84: As men shall wish away—the din of war,
4.85: And murders, and disputes, and banishments,
4.86: And overthrow of towers and waste of cities,
4.87: When Hellas very glorious shall sail
4.88: Over broad Hellespont, and shall convey
4.89: To Phrygia sorrow and to Asia doom.
4.90: And unto Egypt, land of many furrows,
4.91: Shall sorry famine come, and barrenness
4.92: Shall during twenty circling years prevail,
4.93: What time the Nile, corn-nourisher, shall hide
4.94: His dark wave somewhere underneath the earth.
4.95: And there shall come from Asia a great king
4.96: Bearing a spear, with ships innumerable,
4.97: And he shall walk the wet paths of the deep,
4.98: And shall sail after he has cut the mount
4.99: Of lofty summit; him a fugitive
4.100: From battle fearful Asia shall receive.
4.101: And Sicily the wretched shall a stream
4.102: Of powerful fire set all aflame while Etna
4.103: Her flame disgorges; and in the deep chasm
4.104: Down shall the mighty city Croton fall.
4.105: And strife shall be in Hellas; they shall rage
4.106: Against each other, cast down many cities,
4.107: And fighting make an end of many men;
4.108: But equally balanced is the strife with both.
4.109: But, when the race of mortal men shall come
4.110: To the tenth generation, also then
4.111: Upon the Persians shall a servile yoke
4.112: And terror be. But when the Macedonians
4.113: Shall boast the scepter there shall be for Thebes
4.114: An evil conquest from behind, and Carians
4.115: Shall dwell in Tyre, and Tyrians be destroyed.
4.116: And Babylon, great to see but small to fight,
4.117: Shall stand with walls that were in vain hopes built.
4.119: But those from Susa and from Bactria
4.120: Shall all into the land of Hellas flee.
4.121: It shall take place among those yet to be,
4.123: O'erpouring to the sacred isle shall come.
4.124: And Cibyra shall fall and Cyzicus,
4.125: When, earth being shaken by earthquakes, cities fall.
4.126: And sand shall hide all Samos under banks.
4.127: And Delos visible no more, but things
4.128: Of Delos shall all be invisible.
4.129: And to Rhodes shall come evil last, but greatest.
4.130: The Macedonian power shall not abide;
4.131: But from the west a great Italian war
4.132: Shall flourish, under which the world shall bear
4.133: A servile yoke and the Italians serve.
4.134: And thou, O wretched Corinth, thou shalt look
4.135: Sometime upon thy conquest. And thy tower,
4.136: O Carthage, shall press lowly on the ground.
4.138: Shall earthquake lay low, casting headlong down,
4.139: But then, a city firmly set, again
4.140: Shalt stand. O Lycian Myra beautiful,
4.141: Thee never shall the agitated earth
4.142: Set fast; but falling headlong down on earth
4.143: Shalt thon, in manner like an alien, pray
4.144: To flee away into another land,
4.145: When sometime the dark water of the sea
4.146: With thunders and earthquakes shall stop the din
4.148: Also for thee, Armenia, there remains
4.149: A slavish fate; and there shall also come
4.150: To Solyma an evil blast of war
4.151: From Italy, and God's great temple spoil.
4.152: But when these, trusting folly, shall cast off.
4.154: Around the temple, then from Italy
4.155: A mighty king shall like a runaway slave
4.156: Flee over the Euphrates' stream unseen,
4.157: Unknown, who shall some time dare loathsome guilt
4.158: Of matricide, and many other things,
4.159: Having confidence in his most wicked hands.
4.160: And many for the throne shall stain with blood
4.161: Rome's soil while he flees over Parthian land.
4.162: And out of Syria shall come Rome's foremost man,
4.163: Who having burned the temple of Solyma,
4.164: And having slaughtered many of the Jews,
4.165: Shall bring destruction on their great broad land.
4.166: And then too shall an earthquake overthrow
4.167: Both Salamis and Paphos, when dark water
4.168: Shall dash o'er Cyprus washed by many a wave.
4.169: But when from deep cleft of Italian land
4.170: Fire shall come flashing forth in the broad heaven,
4.171: And many cities burn and men destroy,
4.172: And much black ashes shall fill the great sky,
4.173: And small drops like red earth shall fall from heaven,
4.174: Then know the anger of the God of heaven,
4.175: For that they without reason shall destroy
4.176: The nation of the pious. And then strife
4.177: Awakened of war shall come to the West,
4.178: Shall also come the fugitive of Rome,
4.179: Bearing a great spear, having marched across
4.181: O wretched Antioch, they shall call thee
4.182: No more a city when around their spears
4.183: Because of thine own follies thou shalt fall.
4.184: And then on Scyros shall a pestilence
4.186: Alas, alas! O wretched Cyprus, thee
4.187: Shall a broad wave of the sea cover, thee
4.188: Tossed on high by the whirling stormy winds.
4.189: And into Asia there shall come great wealth,
4.190: Which Rome herself once, plundering, put away
4.191: In her luxurious homes; and twice as much
4.192: And more shall she to Asia render back,
4.193: And then there shall be an excess of war.
4.194: And Carian cities by Meander's waters,
4.195: Girded with towers and very beautiful,
4.196: Shall by a bitter famine be destroyed,
4.197: When the Meander his dark water hides.
4.198: But when piety shall perish from mankind,
4.199: And faith and right be hidden in the world,
4.202: And reckless evil deeds, and of the pious
4.203: No one shall make account, but even them all
4.205: In childish folly, in their violence
4.206: Exulting and in blood holding their hands;
4.207: Then know thou that God is no longer mild,
4.208: But gnashing with fury and destroying all
4.209: The race of men by conflagration great.
4.210: Ah! miserable mortals, change these things,
4.211: Nor lead the mighty God to wrath extreme;
4.212: But giving up your swords and pointed knives,
4.214: Wash your whole body in perennial streams,
4.215: And lifting up your hands to heaven seek pardon
4.216: For former deeds and expiate with praise
4.217: Bitter impiety; and God will give
4.218: Repentance; he will not destroy; and wrath
4.219: Will he again restrain, if in your hearts
4.220: Ye all will practice honored piety.
4.221: But if, ill-disposed, ye obey me not,
4.222: But with a fondness for strange lack of sense
4.223: Receive all these things with an evil ear,
4.224: There shall be over all the world a fire
4.225: And greatest omen with sword and with trump
4.226: At sunrise; the whole world shall hear the roar
4.227: And mighty sound. And he shall burn all earth,
4.228: And destroy the whole race of men, and all
4.229: The cities and the rivers and the sea;
4.230: All things he'll burn, and it shall be black dust.
4.231: But when now all things shall have been reduced
4.232: To dust and ashes, and God shall have calmed
4.233: The fire unspeakable which he lit up,
4.234: The bones and ashes of men God himself
4.235: Again will fashion, and he will again
4.236: Raise mortals up, even as they were before.
4.237: And then shall be the judgment, at which God
4.238: Himself as judge shall judge the world again;
4.239: And all who sinned with impious hearts, even them
4.240: Shall he again hide under mounds of earth
4.242: But all who shall be pious shall again
4.243: Live on the earth and (shall inherit there)
4.244: The great immortal God's unwasting bliss,
4.245: God giving spirit life and joy to them
4.246: The pious; and they all shall see themselves.
4.247: Beholding the sun's sweet and cheering light.
4.248: O happy on the earth shall be that man.
5.1: But come, now, hear of me the mournful time
5.2: Of sons of Latium. And first of all,
5.3: After the kings of Egypt were destroyed
5.4: And the like earth had downwards borne them all,
5.5: And after Pella's townsman, under whom
5.6: The whole East and the rich West were cast down,
5.7: Whom Babylon dishonored, and stretched out
5.8: For Philip a dead body (not of Zeus,
5.9: Of Ammon not true things were prophesied),
5.10: And after that one of the race and blood
5.11: Of king Assaracus, who came from Troy,
5.12: Even he who cleft the violence of fire,
5.13: And after many lords, and after men
5.14: To Ares dear, and after the young babes,
5.15: The children of the beast that feeds on sheep,
5.16: The very first lord shall be, who shall sum
5.17: Twice ten with the first letter of his name;
5.18: In wars exceeding powerful shall he be;
5.19: And he shall have the initial sign of ten;
5.20: And in like manner after him to reign
5.21: Is one who has the alphabet's first letter;
5.22: Before him Thrace and Sicily shall crouch,
5.23: Then Memphis, Memphis east headlong to earth
5.24: By reason of the cowardice of rulers
5.25: And of a woman unenslaved who falls
5.26: Upon the wave. And laws will be ordain
5.27: For peoples and put all things under him;
5.28: But after a long time shall he transmit
5.29: His power unto another, who shall have
5.30: Three hundred for his first initial sign,
5.31: And of a river the beloved name,
5.32: And the Persians he shall rule and Babylon;
5.33: And then shall he smite Medians with his spear.
5.34: Then shall one rule who has the initial sign
5.35: Of the number three. And then shall be a lord
5.36: Who shall for first initial have twice ten;
5.37: And he shall come to Ocean's utmost water
5.38: And by Ansonia cleave the refluent tide.
5.39: And one whose mark is fifty shall be lord,
5.40: A dreadful serpent breathing grievous war,
5.41: Who sometime stretching forth his hands shall make
5.42: An end of his own race and stir all things,
5.44: Putting to death and daring countless things;
5.45: And he shall cleave the mountain of two seas
5.46: And sprinkle it with gore; but out of sight
5.47: Shall also vanish the destructive man;
5.48: Then, making himself equal unto God,
5.49: Shall he return; but God will prove him naught.
5.50: And after him shall three kings be destroyed
5.51: By one another. Then a great destroyer
5.52: Of pious men shall come, whom seven times ten
5.53: Shall point out clearly. But from him a son,
5.54: Whom the first letter of three hundred proves,
5.55: Shall take the power. And after him shall be
5.56: A ruler, of the initial sign of four,
5.57: A life-destroyer. Then a reverend man
5.58: Of the number fifty. Next, succeeding him
5.59: Who has the first mark of the initial sign
5.60: Three hundred, shall a Celtic mountaineer,
5.61: Into the strife of battle pressing on,
5.62: Escape not fate unseemly, but shall be
5.63: Worn weary unto death; him foreign dust,
5.64: But dust that of Nemea's flower has name,
5.65: Shall hide a corpse. And after him shall rule
5.66: Another man, with silver helmet decked;
5.67: And unto him shall be the name of a sea;
5.68: And he shall be a man the best of all
5.69: And in all things discreet. And upon thee,
5.70: Thou best of all, above all, dark-haired one,
5.71: And upon thy shoots shall be all these days.
5.72: After him three shall rule; but the third one
5.73: Shall at a late time hold the royal power.
5.74: Worn out am I, thrice-miserable one,
5.75: Sister of Isis, to lay up in heart
5.76: An evil message, and an inspired song
5.77: Of oracles. First Mænades shall dart
5.79: And thou shalt be in evil hands that day
5.80: When the Nile some time shall fill the whole land
5.81: Of Egypt even to sixteen cubits deep;
5.82: It shall wash all the land, and water it
5.83: For mortals; and the pleasure of the land
5.84: Shall be still and the glory of her face.
5.85: Memphis, thou most shalt over Egypt wail;
5.86: For of old ruling mightily the land
5.87: Thou shalt become poor, so that out of heaven
5.88: The Thunderer shall himself with great voice cry:
5.89: "O mighty Memphis, who didst boast of old
5.90: O'er craven mortals greatly, thou shalt wail
5.91: Full of pain and all-hapless, so that thou
5.92: Thyself shalt the eternal God perceive
5.93: Immortal in the clouds. Where among men
5.94: Is now thy mighty pride? Because thou didst
5.96: And didst urge evil forward on good men,
5.97: Thou shalt for such things suffer penalty
5.98: In some like manner. No more openly
5.99: For thee shall there be right among the blessed;
5.100: Fallen from the stars, thou shalt not rise to heaven."
5.101: Now these things unto Egypt God bade me
5.102: Speak out for the last time, when men shall be
5.103: Utterly evil. But they labor hard,
5.104: Evil men evil things awaiting, wrath
5.105: Of the immortal Thunderer in heaven,
5.106: Worshiping stones and beasts instead of God,
5.107: And also fearing many things besides
5.108: Which have no speech, nor mind, nor power to hear;
5.109: Which things it is not right for me to mention,
5.110: Each one an idol, formed by mortal hands;
5.111: Of their own labors and presumptuous thoughts
5.112: Did men receive gods made of wood and stone
5.113: And brass, and gold and silver, foolish too,
5.114: Without life and dumb, molten in the fire
5.115: They made them, vainly trusting such things....
5.116: Thmois and Xois are in sore distress,
5.117: And smitten is the hall of Heracles
5.118: And Zeus and Hermes (king). And as for thee,
5.120: (Of cities) war shall not leave, nor (plague)...
5.121: For thy pride thou shalt pay as many things
5.122: As thou before didst. Silent shalt thou be
5.123: A long age, and the day of thy return...
5.125: No more for thee shall flow luxurious drink...
5.127: And artful men, with blood and corpses....
5.128: By sacred altars one of barbarous mind,
5.129: Strong, full of blood and raging senselessly,
5.130: With countless numbers rushing to destruction,
5.131: And then shalt thou, in cities very rich,
5.132: Be very weary. Falling on the earth
5.133: All Asia shall wail on account of gifts
5.134: Crowning her head with which she was by thee
5.135: Delighted. But, as he himself obtained
5.136: The Persian land by lot, he shall make war
5.137: And killing every man destroy all life,
5.138: So that there shall remain for wretched mortals
5.139: A third part. But with nimble leap shall he
5.140: Himself speed from the West, and all the land
5.141: Besiege and waste. But when he shall possess
5.142: The height of power and odious reverence,
5.143: He shall come, wishing to destroy the city
5.144: Even of the blessed. And a certain king
5.145: Sent forth from God against him shall destroy
5.146: All mighty kings and bravest men. And thus
5.147: Shall judgment by the Immortal come to men,
5.148: Alas, alas for thee, unhappy heart!
5.149: Why dost thou move me to declare these things,
5.150: The painful rule of Egypt over many?
5.151: Go to the East, to races of the Persians
5.152: Who lack in understanding, and show them
5.153: That which is now and that which is to be.
5.154: The river of Euphrates shall bring on
5.155: A deluge, and it shall destroy the Persians,
5.157: And the Massagetæ that relish war
5.158: And trust in bows. All Asia fire-ablaze
5.159: Shall to the isles beam brightly. Pergamos,
5.160: Revered of old, shall perish from its base,
5.161: And Pitane among men shall appear
5.162: All-desolate. All Lesbos shall sink deep
5.163: Into the deep, and thus shall be destroyed.
5.164: Smyrna, whirled down her cliffs, shall wail aloud,
5.165: She that was once revered and given a name
5.167: Shall over their own country, then reduced
5.168: To ashes, wail, and o'er great Syria,
5.169: And o'er Phoenicia that has many tribes.
5.170: Alas, alas for thee, O Lycia;
5.171: How many evils does the sea contrive
5.172: Against thee, mounting up of its own will
5.173: Upon the painful land! And it shall dash
5.174: With evil earthquake and with bitter streams
5.175: On the rough Lycian land that once breathed perfume.
5.176: And there shall be for Phrygia fearful wrath
5.177: Because of sorrow for which Rhea came,
5.178: Mother of Zeus, and there continued long.
5.179: The sea shall overthrow the Centaur race
5.180: And barbarous nation, and beneath the earth
5.181: Shall tear away the Lapithæan land.
5.182: The river of deep eddies and deep flow,
5.184: Snatching men from the earth. Eridanus
5.185: (Pretending once to bear the forms of beasts).
5.186: Hellas thrice wretched shall the poets weep,
5.187: When one from Italy shall smite the neck
5.188: Of the isthmus, mighty king of mighty Rome,
5.189: A man made equal to God, whom, they say,
5.190: Zeus himself and the august Hera bore;
5.191: He, courting by his voice all-musical
5.192: Applause for his sweet songs, shall put to death
5.193: With his own wretched mother many men.
5.194: From Babylon shall flee the fearful lord
5.195: And shameless whom all mortals and best men
5.196: Abhor; for he slew many and laid hands
5.197: Upon the womb; against his wives he sinned
5.198: And of men stained with blood had he been formed.
5.199: And he shall come to monarchs of the Medes
5.200: And Persians, first whom he loved and to whom
5.201: He brought renown, while with those wicked men
5.202: He lurked against a nation not desired;
5.203: And on the temple made by God he seized
5.204: And citizens and people going in,
5.205: Of whom I justly sang the praise, he burned;
5.206: For when this man appeared the whole creation
5.207: Was shaken and kings perished—and yet power
5.208: Remained among them, and they quite destroyed
5.209: The mighty city and the righteous people.
5.210: But when the fourth year a great star shall shine,
5.211: Which alone shall the whole earth overpower
5.212: Because of honor, which was first assigned
5.213: To lord Poseidon; then a great star shall come
5.214: From heaven into the dreadful sea and burn
5.215: The vasty deep, and Babylon itself,
5.216: And the land of Italy, because of which
5.217: There perished many holy faithful men
5.218: Among the Hebrews and a people true.
5.219: Thou shalt be among evil mortals made
5.220: To suffer evils, but thou shalt remain
5.222: Hating thy soil; for thou didst have desire
5.223: For sorcery, adulteries were with thee
5.224: And lawless carnal intercourse with boys,
5.227: Thou city of the Latin land, unclean
5.228: In all things, Mænad having joy in snakes,
5.229: Over thy hanks a widow shalt thou sit
5.230: And the river Tiber shall lament for thee,
5.231: His consort thee, who hast a blood-stained heart
5.232: And impious soul. Didst thou not understand
5.233: What God can do, and what he doth devise?
5.234: But thou saidst, "I'm alone, and me no one
5.235: Shall sack," But now shall God, who ever is,
5.236: Thee and all thine destroy, and in that land
5.237: No longer shall thy ensign yet remain,
5.238: As of old, when the mighty God received
5.239: Thy honors. Stay, O lawless one, alone,
5.240: And mixed with burning fire inhabit thou
5.241: In Hades the Tartarean lawless land.
5.242: And now again, O Egypt, I bewail
5.243: Thy blind delusion; Memphis, first in toils,
5.244: Thou shalt be filled up with the dead; in thee
5.245: The pyramids shall speak a ruthless sound.
5.246: O Python, who wast justly called of old
5.247: The double city, be for ages silent,
5.248: So that thou mayest cease from wickedness.
5.249: Reckless in evils, treasury of toils,
5.251: Much-weeping, thou a widow shalt remain
5.252: Through all time. Thou didst full of years become
5.253: While thou alone wast ruling o'er the world;
5.254: But when the white dress Barca round herself
5.255: Shall put on over that which is defiled,
5.256: Would that I neither were nor had been born!
5.257: O Thebes, where is thy great strength? A fierce man
5.258: Shall slay the people; but thou, wretched one,
5.259: Grasping thy dusky dress shalt wail alone,
5.260: And thou shalt make atonement for all things.
5.261: Which thou aforetime with a shameless soul
5.262: Didst perpetrate. They also shall behold.
5.263: A mourning on account of lawless deeds.
5.264: And a mighty man of the Ethiopians
5.265: Shall overthrow Syene; by their might
5.267: Pentapolis, a man of mighty strength
5.268: Shall burn thee whole. All-tearful Libya,
5.269: Who shall explain thy follies? And Cyrene,
5.270: Of mortals who shall pitiably weep
5.271: For thee? Thou shalt not even to the time
5.272: Of thy destruction cease thy hateful wail.
5.273: Among the Britons and among the Gauls,
5.274: Rich in gold, Ocean shall be roaring loud
5.275: Filled with much blood; for evil things
5.276: Did they unto God's children, when a king
5.277: Of the Sidonians, a Phoenician, led
5.278: A mighty Gallic host from Syria;
5.279: And he shall slaughter thee, thyself, Ravenna,
5.280: And unto slaughter shall he lead the way.
5.282: Together fear; for when with these the course
5.283: Of Capricorn and Taurus in the Twins
5.284: Shall wind about the middle of the heaven,
5.285: Virgo then rising, and about his front.
5.286: Fastening a belt the sun shall lead all heaven,
5.287: There shall be moving downwards to the earth
5.288: A mighty conflagration high in air,
5.289: And a new nature in the warlike stars,
5.290: So that the whole land of the Ethiops
5.291: Shall perish in the midst of fire and groans.
5.292: And weep thou, Corinth, the destruction sad
5.293: Which is in thee; for when with pliant threads
5.294: The Fates, three sisters, spinning shall aloft
5.295: Lead him who flees by guile against the voice
5.296: Of the isthmus, until all shall look at him
5.297: Who once cut out the rock with ductile brass,
5.298: He also shall destroy and smite thy land,
5.299: As it bath been appointed. For to him
5.300: God gave strength to accomplish that which could
5.301: No earlier of all the kings together.
5.302: And first with sickle cleaving off the roots
5.303: From three heads he shall give food in excess
5.304: To others, so that kings unclean shall eat
5.305: The flesh of parents. For unto all men
5.306: Slaughter and terrors are laid up in store
5.307: Because of the great city and just people
5.308: Saved through all time, whom Providence held high.
5.309: O thou unstable one and ill-advised,
5.310: By evil fates surrounded, for mankind
5.311: Both a beginning and great end of toil,—
5.312: Of suffering creation and of part
5.314: Of evils, and for men a great curse, who
5.315: Of mortals wished for thee? Who has not been
5.316: Embittered from within? Cast down in thee
5.317: A king his honored life lost. Evilly
5.318: Hast thou disposed all things and washed away
5.319: All that is fair, and by thee have been changed
5.320: The world's fair folds. In strife with us perhaps
5.321: Thou hast brought forward these unstable things;
5.322: And how dost thou say, "I will thee persuade,"
5.323: And "If in any thing thou blame me, speak?"
5.324: There was once among men the sun's bright light
5.325: The prophets' common ray being spread abroad;
5.326: Speech dripping honey, fair drink for all men,
5.327: Appeared and grew, and day arose on all.
5.328: Because of this, thou narrow-minded one
5.329: Leader of greatest evils, both a sword
5.330: And grief shall come in that day. For mankind
5.331: Both a beginning and great end of toil,—
5.332: Of suffering creation and of part
5.333: Restored again,—hear, O thou curse of men,
5.335: But when the Persian land shall keep away
5.336: From war and plague and groaning, in that day
5.337: A race divine of blessed heavenly Jews
5.338: Shall offer prayer, who shall dwell round about
5.339: God's city in mid portions of the land,
5.340: And even as far as Joppa building round
5.341: A great wall they shall carry it aloft
5.342: Unto the gloomy clouds. No more shall trump
5.343: Sound battle-din nor by a foe's mad hands
5.344: Shall they be cut off; but they shall set up
5.345: Their trophies for an age of evil men.
5.346: And one shall come again from heaven, a man
5.347: Preeminent, whose hands on fruitful tree
5.348: By far the noblest of the Hebrews stretched,
5.349: Who at one time did make the sun stand still
5.350: When he spoke with fair word and holy lips.
5.351: No longer vex thy soul within thy breast
5.352: By reason of the sword, rich child of God,
5.353: Flower longed for by him only, goodly light
5.354: And noble branch, a scion much beloved,
5.356: Inspired by hymns, No more shall unclean foot
5.357: Of Greeks keep revel round about thy land,
5.358: Who held within their breast a lawless mind;
5.359: But thee shall glorious children honor much
5.360: And be expert in songs and holy tongues,
5.361: With sacrifices of all kinds and prayers
5.362: Honored of God. All who endure the toils
5.363: Of small affliction and the just shall have
5.365: But the wicked, who to heaven sent lawless speech,
5.366: Shall cease their speaking one against another,
5.367: And hide themselves until the world be changed.
5.368: And there shall be a rain of gleaming fire
5.369: From the clouds; and no more shall mortals reap
5.370: The fair corn from the earth; all things unsown
5.371: And unplowed, until mortal men shall know
5.372: The Lord of all things, the immortal God
5.373: Always existing, and no more revere
5.374: Mortal things, neither dogs nor vultures' nests,
5.375: And what things Egypt taught to magnify
5.376: With dumb mouths and dull lips. But all these things
5.377: The holy land of the only pious men
5.378: Shall bring forth, from the honey-dripping rock
5.379: A stream and from a spring ambrosial milk
5.380: Shall flow for all the just; for in one God,
5.381: One Father, who alone is glorious,
5.382: Having great piety and faith they hoped.
5.383: But why does the wise mind grant me these things?
5.384: And now thee, wretched Asia, piteously
5.385: I mourn and the race of Ionians
5.386: And Carians and Lydians rich in gold.
5.387: Alas, alas for thee, O Sardis; and alas
5.388: For Trallis much beloved; alas, alas,
5.390: Thus shalt thou be by earthquakes overthrown
5.391: And ruined, and be also changed to dust.
5.394: By chasms and earthquakes shall come headlong down.
5.395: Sometime into the dreadful sea, as storms
5.397: Shall wail aloud, lament beside her banks,
5.398: And for her temple search which is no more.
5.399: And then ineensed shall God the imperishable,
5.400: Who dwells on high, burl thunderbolts from heaven
5.401: Down on the head of him that is impure.
5.402: And in the place of winter there shall be
5.403: In that day summer. And to mortal men
5.404: Shall then be great woe; for the Thunderer
5.405: Shall utterly destroy all shameless men
5.406: And with his thunders and with lightning-flames
5.407: And blazing thunderbolts men of ill-will,
5.408: And thus shall he destroy the impious ones,
5.409: So that there shall remain upon the earth
5.410: Dead bodies more in number than the sand.
5.411: For Smyrna also, weeping her Lycurgus,
5.412: Shall come unto the gates of Ephesus
5.413: And she herself shall perish even more.
5.414: And foolish Cyme with her inspired streams
5.415: Cast down by hands of godless men unjust
5.416: And lawless, shall to heaven not so much
5.417: As a word utter; but she shall remain
5.418: Dead in Cymæan streams. And then shall they
5.420: Cyme's rough populace and shameless tribe,
5.421: Having a sign, shall know for what they toiled.
5.422: And then, when they shall have bewailed their land
5.426: Alas for thee, cease from thy revelry.
5.428: With riches mixed, what thou hast longed to have
5.429: Thou shalt have, even a land of many tears,
5.430: Since thou wast angry towards a land beside
5.432: Beside the waters of Meander, thee
5.433: Shall by the nightly surges under shore
5.434: God's wrath and foresight utterly destroy.
5.435: Take me not, willing, to the neighboring land
5.436: Of Phœbus; sometime shall a thunderbolt
5.438: Because she seized on Phœbus' crafty song
5.439: And the wise care and prudent plan of men.
5.440: Father of all, be gracious to the land
5.441: Of Judah, well fed, fruit-abounding, great,
5.442: In order that thy judgments we may see.
5.443: For thou, O God, in kindness didst regard
5.444: This land first that it might appear to be
5.445: Thy gracious gift unto all mortal men
5.446: And to hold fast what God put in their charge.
5.447: The works thrice wretched of the Thracians
5.448: I yearn to see, and wall between two seas
5.449: Trailed in the dust along beneath the mist,
5.450: Even like a river for the swimming fish.
5.451: O wretched Hellespont, sometime a child
5.452: Of the Assyrians shall throw a yoke
5.453: Across thee; battle of the Thracians comes
5.454: And shall despoil thy strength. And there shall rule
5.456: A king of Egypt, and a barbarous clime
5.457: Shall waste the strength of captains. Lydians
5.459: With the Pisidians, all equipped for war
5.460: Shall in a mass bring evil strife to pass.
5.461: Thrice wretched Italy, thou shalt remain
5.463: By deadly sting to perish utterly.
5.464: And sometime high in the broad heaven above
5.465: Like thunder-roaring shall God's voice be heard.
5.466: And the unwasting flames of the sun himself
5.467: Shall be no more, nor shall the brilliant light
5.468: Of the moon again be in the latest time,
5.469: When God shall be the ruler. And dark gloom
5.470: Shall be o'er all the earth, and blinded men
5.471: And evil beasts and woe; that day shall be
5.472: A long time, so that men shall see that God
5.473: Himself is Lord, the overseer of all
5.474: In front of heaven. And then will he himself
5.475: Not pity hostile men, who sacrifice
5.476: Their herds of lambs and sheep and calves and goats
5.477: And bellowing golden-horned bulls, offering them
5.478: To lifeless Hermæ and to gods of stone.
5.479: But let the law of wisdom be your guide
5.480: And the glory of the righteous; lest sometime
5.482: Each race of men and shameless tribe of life,
5.483: It doth behoove them faithfully to love.
5.484: The Father, the wise God who ever is.
5.485: In the last time, at the turning of the moon,
5.486: There shall be raging through the world a war
5.487: And carried on with cunning and in guile.
5.488: And from the limits of the earth shall come
5.489: Fleeing, and pondering sharp things in his mind,
5.490: A matricidal man who every land
5.491: Shall overpower and over all things rule,
5.492: And see all things more wisely than all men;
5.493: And that for whose sake he himself was slain
5.494: Shall he seize forthwith. And he shall destroy
5.495: Many men and great tyrants and shall burn
5.496: All of them, as none other ever did,
5.497: And he shall raise up them that are afraid
5.498: For emulation's sake. And from the West
5.499: Much war shall come to men, and blood shall flow
5.500: Down hill till it becomes deep-eddying streams.
5.501: And in the plains of Macedonia
5.502: Shall wrath distil and give help from the West,
5.503: But to the king destruction. And a wind
5.504: Of winter then shall blow upon the earth,
5.505: And the plain be filled with evil war again.
5.506: For fire shall rain down from the heavenly plains.
5.507: On mortals, and therewith blood, water, flash
5.508: Of lightning, murky darkness, night in heaven,
5.509: And waste in war and o'er the slaughter mist,
5.510: And these together shall destroy all kings
5.511: And noblest men. Thus shall be made to cease
5.512: Then the destruction pitiable of war.
5.513: And no more shall one fight with swords or iron
5.514: Or even darts, which things shall not again
5.515: Be lawful. But wise people shall have peace,

12.230: Of the first unit shall another king
12.231: Bear the rule; and another after him
12.232: Shall be commander, of tens numbering seven;

12.242: Training his hands—excuse itself of death.
12.243: And after him another man shall rule,

12.246: Of the first unit; and he shall be good
