12.264: Of the great pious king; and at the end
12.265: Of his life, having then proclaimed his son
12.266: Succeeding to the kingdom, he shall die
12.267: By his own lot and leave the royal power
12.268: Unto the ruler with the golden hair,
12.269: Who with two tens in his name, born a king
12.270: From the race of his father, shall receive
12.271: Dominion. This man with superior powers
12.272: Of mind shall grasp all things; and he shall rival
12.274: And be the best in mighty arms and have
12.275: The greatest fame in chase and horsemanship;
12.276: But he shall live in peril all alone.
12.277: And while this man is ruler there shall be
12.278: A fearful sign: there shall be a great mist
12.279: Then in the plain of Rome, so that a man
12.280: May not discern his neighbor. And then wars
12.281: Shall come to pass along with mournful cares,
12.282: When the king himself, exceeding mad with love,
12.283: And weakly, shall come in the marriage-bod
12.286: And then, in helpless loneliness concealed,
12.287: The mighty baneful man held under wrath
12.288: Shall in a bath-room suffer evil plight,
12.289: Man-slaying Ares bound by treacherous fate.
12.290: Know then the fatal lot of Rome is near
12.291: Because of zeal for power; and by the hands
12.292: Of Ares many in Palladian halls
12.293: Shall perish. And then Rome shall be bereft
12.294: And shall repay all things, which she alone
12.295: Before accomplished by her many wars.
12.296: My heart laments, my heart within me mourns;
12.297: For from the time when thy first king, proud Rome,
12.298: Gave good law to thee and to men on earth,
12.299: And the Word of the great immortal God
12.300: Came to the earth, until the nineteenth reign
12.301: Shall have been finished Cronos shall complete
12.302: Two hundred years, twice twenty and twice two,
12.303: With six months added; then the twentieth king,
12.304: When smitten with sharp brass he with the sword
12.305: Shall in thy houses pour out blood, shall make
12.306: Thy race a widow, having in his name
12.307: The letter which the number eighty shows,
12.308: And burdened with old age; but he shall make
12.309: A widow of thee in a little time,
12.311: And murders, homicides, and deadly feuds
12.312: And miseries of conquests there shall be,
12.313: And in confusion many a horse and man
12.314: Shall, cleft by force of hands, fall in the plain.
12.315: And then another man shall rule, and have
12.316: The sign of his name in the number ten;
12.317: And many sorrows shall he bring to pass,
12.318: And groans, and he shall plunder many men;
12.319: But he himself shall be short-lived and fall
12.320: By mighty Ares, struck by gleaming iron.
12.321: Another, numbering fifty, then shall come,
12.322: A warrior roused up by the East for rule;
12.323: A warlike Ares he shall come to Thrace;
12.324: And he shall flee thereafter and shall come
12.325: Into the land of the Bithynians
12.326: And the Cilician plain; but brazen Ares
12.327: The life-destroyer shall with speedy stroke
12.328: Utterly spoil him in the Assyrian fields.
12.329: And then again there shall rule craftily
12.330: A man skilled in fraud, full of various wiles,
12.331: Roused up by the West, and his name shall have
12.332: The number of two hundred. And again
12.333: Another sign: he shall contrive a war
12.334: For royal power against Assyrian men,
12.335: Raise a whole army and subject all things.
12.336: And he shall rule the Romans with his might;
12.337: But there is much contrivance in his heart,
12.338: Impulse of baleful Ares; serpent dire,
12.339: And violent in war, who shall destroy
12.340: All high-born men upon the earth, and slay
12.341: The noble for their wealth, and, robber like,
12.342: Stripping all earth while men are perishing,
12.343: He shall go to the East; and all deceit
12.346: Having the name of a puissant lord
12.347: Of Macedon, by the first letter known;
12.348: Bringing in broils around him he shall flee
12.349: The hard deception of the coming king
12.350: In the bosom of the army; but the one
12.351: Who rules by his barbaric usages,
12.353: Slain by strong Arcs with the gleaming iron;
12.354: Him even dead shall people tear in pieces.
12.355: And then the kings of Persia shall rise up;
12.357: And Phrygia shall with earthquakes groan again
12.360: For you first once the yawning oarth received.
12.363: Shall wail... while men are perishing
12.364: In the hands of Ares; and the lot of men
12.365: Shall be bad; but then by the eastern way
12.366: Hastening to look down upon Italy,
12.367: Stripped naked ho shall fall by gleaming iron,
12.368: Acquiring hatred for his mother's sake,
12.369: For seasons are of all sorts; each holds back
12.370: The other... gleaming... and this not at once all know;
12.371: For all things shall not be (the lot) of all,
12.372: But only those shall be for happiness
12.373: Who honor God and shun idolatry.
12.374: And now, Lord of the world, of every realm.
12.375: Unfeigned immortal King—for thou didst put
12.376: Into my heart the oracle divine—

12.378: What things I say; for thou art in me he

12.380: A little and put from my heart aside
12.381: The charming song; for weary is my heart
12.382: Foretelling with divine words royal power.

13.68: By reason of the strife of shameful men.
13.69: Strong men who were aforetime terrible
13.70: Being then impotent shall pray for peace
13.71: By reason of the wickedness of chiefs.
13.72: And there shall come wrath of the mighty God

14.1: O men, why do ye vainly think on things
14.2: Too lofty, as if ye immortal were?
14.3: And ye are ruling but a little time,
14.4: And over mortals all desire to reign,
14.5: Not understanding that God himself hates
14.6: The lust of rule, and most of all things hates
14.8: And over them he stirs up what is dark;

14.76: And the other with three hundred shall partake,
14.77: Cruel, who gold and silver in much fire.
14.78: Shall melt in statues of gods made with hands,
14.79: And to the armies they, equipped for war,
14.80: Will, for the sake of victory, moneys give,
14.81: Dividing many costly things and goods;
14.82: And in like manner, striving eagerly
14.83: After power, they shall harm disastrously
14.84: The arrow-shooting Parthians of the deep
14.85: And swift Euphrates, and the hostile Medes,
14.88: But when the king shall his own fate unloose
14.89: Leaving unto his sons more fit for arms
14.90: The royal scepter and entreating right,
14.91: Then they, forgetful of their father's words
14.92: And having their hands all prepared for war,
14.93: Shall rush in conflict for the royal power.
14.94: And then another lord, of the third number,
14.95: Shall rule alone, and smitten by a sword
14.96: Shall quickly see his fate. Then after him
14.97: Shall many perish at each other's hands,
14.98: Being very valiant for the royal power.
14.99: Moreover a great-hearted one shall rule
14.100: The very mighty Romans, an old lord,
14.101: Of the number four, and manage all things well.
14.102: And then upon Phoenicia shall come war
14.103: And conflict, when there shall come nations near
14.104: Of arrow-shooting Persians; ah, how many
14.105: Shall before men of barbarous speech fall down!
14.107: The loudly-boasting shall bebold each other
14.108: Amid the blood and bodies of the dead.
14.110: Thou shalt a great and unsuccessful war
14.111: Stir up through the impiety of men.
14.112: Ah, hapless Tyrians, ye shall gather in
14.113: An evil harvest; when in the day-time
14.114: The sun that lighteth mortals shall withdraw,
14.115: And his disk not appear, and drops of blood
14.116: Thiek and abundant shall flow down from heaven
14.117: Upon the earth. And then the king shall die,
14.118: Betrayed by his companions. After him
14.119: Shall many shameless leaders still promote
14.120: The wicked strife and one another kill.
14.121: And then shall there a reverend ruler be,
14.122: Of much skill, with a name that numbers five,
14.123: Confiding in great armies, whom mankind
14.124: Will fondly love because of royal power;
14.125: And having the good name he shall thereto
14.126: Add by good deeds. But while he reigns there shall
14.128: A fearful sign. From the Cilieian land
14.129: A city new and beautiful and strong
14.130: Shall by the deep strong rivers be destroyed.
14.131: And in Propontis and in Phrygia
14.132: Shall there be many earthquakes. And the king
14.133: Of great renown shall under his own lot
14.134: By wasting deadly sickness lose his life.
14.135: And after him shall rule two lordly kings,
14.136: One numbering three hundred, and one three;
14.137: And many shall he utterly destroy
14.138: In defense of the seven-hill city Rome,
14.139: And for the sake of powerful sovereignty.
14.140: And then shall evil to the senate come,
14.141: Nor shall it from the angry king escape
14.142: While he holds wrath against it. And a sign
14.143: Shall then appear to all men upon earth;
14.144: And fuller shall the rains be, snow and hail
14.145: Shall ruin field-fruits o'er the boundless earth.
14.146: But they shall fall in wars, slain by strong Ares
14.147: In behalf of the war for the Italians.
14.148: And then again another king shall rule,
14.149: Full of devices, gathering all the army,
14.150: And for the sake of war distributing
14.151: Money to those with brazen breastplate clad;
14.152: But thereupon shall Nilus, rich in corn,
14.154: For two years the dark soil and fruitful land
14.155: Of Egypt; but all things shall famine seize
14.156: And war and robbers, murders, homicides.
14.157: And many cities shall by warlike men
14.158: Be thrown down headlong by the army's hands;
14.159: And he, betrayed, shall fall by gleaming iron.
14.160: After him one whose number is three hundred
14.161: Shall rule the Romans, very mighty men;
14.162: He shall stretch forth a life-destroying spear
14.163: Against the Armenians and the Parthians,
14.164: The Assyrians and the Persians firm in war.
14.165: And then anew shall a creation be
14.166: Of splendidly built Rome with gold and amber
14.167: And silver and ivory in order raised;
14.168: And in her many people shall abide
14.169: From all the East and from the prosperous West;
14.170: And the king shall make other laws for her;
14.171: But then shall death destructive and strong fate
14.172: In turn receive him in a boundless isle.
14.173: And there shall rule another, of ten triads,
14.174: A man like a wild beast, fair-haired and grim,
14.175: Who shall be a descendant of the Greeks.
14.176: And then a city of Molossian Phthia
14.177: Feeding much, and Larissa shall be bent
14.179: And then too in horse-feeding Scythia
14.180: Shall be an insurrection. And dire war
14.181: Shall be hard by the waters of the lake
14.182: Mæotis at streams by the utmost month
14.183: Of the fount of watery Phasis on the mead
14.184: Of asphodel; and there shall many fall
14.185: By powerful warriors. Ah, how many men
14.186: Shall Ares with strong brass receive! And then,
14.187: Having destroyed a Scythian race, the king
14.188: Shall die in his own lot unloosing life.
14.189: And yet another of the number four
14.190: Shall rule thereafter, openly made known
14.191: A dreadful man, whom all Armenians,
14.192: Who drink the best ice of the flowing stream
14.193: Araxes, and the Persians of great soul
14.194: Shall fear in wars. And between Colchians
14.195: And very strong Pelasgi there shall be
14.196: Wars, fights, and homicides. And those who hold
14.197: The cities of the land of Phrygia
14.198: And those of the Propontis, and make bare
14.199: From out their scabbards the two-edged swords,
14.200: Shall smite each other through sore impiousness.
14.201: And then shall God to mortal men display
14.202: From heaven a great sign with the rolling years,
14.203: A bat, the portent of bad war to come.
14.204: And then the king shall not escape stern fate,
14.205: But die by hand, slain by the gleaming iron.
14.206: After him, numbering fifty, there shall rule
14.207: Again another coming out of Asia,
14.208: A dreadful terror, fighting hand to hand;
14.209: And he shall set war on Rome's stately walls,
14.212: By Euxine sea, at Thraeia's sandy bay.
14.213: And then the king shall not escape stern fate,
14.214: And they will tear in pieces his dead corpse.
14.215: And then, the king slain, man-ennobling Rome
14.216: Shall be a desert, and much people perish.
14.217: And then again one terrible and dread.
14.218: From mighty Egypt shall rule, and destroy
14.219: Great hearted Parthians and Medes and Germans,
14.222: That bear the quiver, bent Massagetæ,
14.223: And Persians thinking themselves more than men.
14.224: And then a famous man shall look upon
14.225: All Hellas, acting as an enemy
14.227: And there shall be a dread sign while he rules:
14.228: Crowns altogether like the shining stars
14.229: Shall from heaven in the south and north appear.
14.230: And then shall he bequeath the royal power
14.231: To his son whose initial letter heads
14.232: The alphabet, when in the halls of Hades.
14.233: The manly king in his own lot shall go.
14.234: But when the son of this man in the land
14.235: Of Rome shall rule, shown by the number one,
14.236: There shall be over all the earth great peace
14.237: Much longed for, and the Latins will love him.
14.238: As king because of his own father's worth;
14.239: Him, eager to go both to East and West,
14.240: The Roman people shall against his will
14.241: Retain at home and in command of Rome,
14.242: For among all there is a friendly heart
14.243: Felt for their royal and illustrious lord.
14.244: But baneful death shall snatch him out of life,
14.246: But others afterwards again shall smite
14.247: Each other, powerful warriors, carrying on
14.248: An evil strife, not holding kingly power,
14.249: But being tyrants. And in all the world
14.250: Shall they bring many evil things to pass,
14.251: But chiefly for the Romans till the time
14.252: Of the third Dionysus, until armed
14.253: With helmet Ares shall from Egypt come,
14.254: Whom they shall surname Dionysus lord.
14.255: But when the famous royal purple cloak
14.256: A murderous lion and murderous lioness
14.257: Shall rend, together they shall grasp the lungs
14.258: Of the changed kingdom; then a holy king,
14.259: Whose name has the first letter, pressing hard
14.260: For victory, shall cast down hostile chiefs
14.261: To be the food of dogs and birds of prey.
14.262: Alas for thee, O city burned with fire,
14.263: O powerful Rome! How many things must thou
14.264: Needs suffer when all these things come to pass!
14.265: But the great far-famed king shall afterward
14.266: Raise thee all up again with gold and amber
14.267: And silver and ivory, and in the world
14.268: Thou shalt in thy possessions foremost be,
14.270: And race-grounds; and then shalt thou be again
14.271: A light for all, even as thou wast before.
14.273: And the Laconians, who are situate
14.275: Thick grown with rushes, Tricca and Dodona,
14.277: Around the summit of Olympian mount,
14.279: But when God shall for mortals bring to pass
14.280: A great sign, day dark twilight round the world,
14.281: Even then to thee, O king, the end shall come,
14.282: Nor is it possible that thou escape
14.283: A brother's piercing dart against thee hurled.
14.284: And then again shall rule a life-destroyer,
14.285: A fiery eagle from the royal race,
14.286: Who shall of Egypt's offspring take fast hold,
14.287: Younger, but than his brother much more strong,
14.288: Who has for his first sign the number eighty.
14.289: And then the whole world shall for honor's sake
14.290: Bear in its lap the soul-distressing wrath
14.291: Of the immortal God; and there shall come
14.292: On mortal men, the creatures of a day,
14.293: Famines and plagues and wars and homicides,
14.294: And an incessant darkness o'er the earth,
14.295: Mother of peoples, and relentless wrath
14.296: From heaven, and disorder of the times,
14.297: And earthquake shocks, and flaming thunderbolts,
14.298: And stones and storms of rain and squalid drops.
14.299: And the high summits of the Phrygian land
14.300: Feel the shock, bases of the Scythian hills
14.301: Feel the shock, cities tremble, and all earth
14.302: Trembles at the cliffs of the land of Greece.
14.303: And many cities, God being very wroth,
14.304: Shall fall prone under burning thunderbolts
14.305: And with bewailings, and to shun the wrath
14.306: And make escape is not even possible.
14.307: And then the king shall by a strong hand fall,
14.308: Struck as if he were no one by his men.
14.309: After him of the Latins many men
14.310: Wearing the purple mantle on their shoulders
14.311: Shall be again raised up, who shall by lot
14.312: Desire to lay hold on the royal power.
14.313: And then upon the stately walls of Rome
14.314: Shall be three kings, two having the first number,
14.315: And one the eponym of victory
14.316: Bearing as no one else. They shall love Rome.
14.317: And all the world, concerned for mortal men;
14.318: But they shall not accomplish anything;
14.319: For God has not been gracious to the world
14.320: Neither will be be gentle with mankind,
14.321: Because they have done many evil things.
14.322: Therefore to kings shall he a mean soul bring
14.323: Still worse than that of leopards and of wolves;
14.324: For harshly seizing them with their own hands,
14.325: Like feeble women who are idly slain,
14.326: Shall men in brazen breastplate utterly
14.327: Destroy the kings together with their scepters.
14.328: Ah, wretched lofty men of glorious Rome,
14.329: Trusting in false oaths ye shall be destroyed.
14.330: And then shall many masters with the spear,
14.331: Men rushing not in order furious on,
14.332: Take away offspring of the first-born men
14.334: Shall the Most High then bring on dreadful doom,
14.335: And all men with their works shall he destroy.
14.336: But into judgment yet again shall God
14.337: Cause them to come that have a shameless soul,
14.338: As many as determined evil things;
14.339: And they themselves are fenced in, falling one
14.340: Upon another, and given over there.
14.343: All one by one, yet a brilliant comet
14.345: Of much to come, of war and battle strife.
14.346: But at the time when one about the isles
14.347: And grievous harm of temples, he shall bid
14.348: One in great haste to gather in Rome's halls
14.349: For twelve months wheat and barley in abundance,
14.350: And this most quickly. And in wretched plight
14.351: The city shall be those days, and straightway
14.352: Shall it again be prosperous not a little;
14.353: And rest shall be when that rule is destroyed.
14.354: And then the last race of the Latin kings
14.355: Shall be, and after it again shall grow
14.356: Dominion, children and the children's race
14.357: Shall be unshaken; for it shall be known,
14.358: Since of a surety God himself is king.
14.359: There is a land dear, nourisher of men,
14.360: Situate in a plain, and round it Nile
14.361: Marks off the boundary and separates
14.363: And Syrians short-lived, one from one place,
14.364: Another from another, from that land
14.365: Shall snatch away all movable effects;
14.366: A great and careful lord shall be their king,
14.367: Training up youth and sending off for men,
14.368: And planning something fearful about those
14.369: Most fearful, above all he shall send forth
14.370: A powerful helper of all Italy
14.371: The lofty-minded. And when he shall come
14.372: Unto the dark sea of Assyria
14.373: He shall despoil Phoenicians in their homes,
14.374: And fastening evil war and battle dire
14.375: Shall be one lord of the two lords of earth.
14.376: And now will I for Alexandrians sing
14.378: Shall possess sacred Egypt, land unharmed,
14.379: Unshaken, when wrath from the gods shall come.
14.381: Then shall the oracles be all fulfilled.
14.382: But when three youths in the Olympian games
14.383: Shall conquer, and thou shalt bid them that know
14.384: The oracles that call on God to cleanse
14.385: First by the blood of sucking quadruped,
14.386: Thrice therefore shall the Most High then bring on
14.387: A fearful lot, and he shall over all
14.388: Brandish the mournful long spear; then much blood
14.389: Barbarian shall be poured out in the dust.
14.390: When the city shall be plundered utterly
14.392: Who is dead, also happy any one
14.393: Who is without a child; for he who once
14.394: Was leader surnamed for them that are free,
14.395: Far-famed in song, no longer in his mind
14.396: Revolving earlier plans, shall place their neck
14.397: Under a servile yoke; such slavery,
14.398: Cause of much weeping, shall a lord impose.
14.399: And then straightway an army of Sicilians
14.401: When a barbarian nation shall again
14.402: Come suddenly; and the fruit, when it grows,
14.403: They from the field shall sever. Upon them
14.404: Shall God the lofty Thunderer bestow
14.406: Shall stranger pluck from stranger hateful gold.
14.407: But now when all shall look upon the blood
14.408: Of the flesh-eating lion and there comes
14.409: Upon the body a murderous lioness,
14.410: Down from his head will he the scepter cast
14.411: Away from him. And as in friendly feast
14.412: In Egypt when the people all partake,
14.413: They perform valiant deeds, and one restrains
14.414: Another, and among them there is much
14.415: Shouting aloud; so also shall there be
14.416: Upon mankind the fear of furious strife,
14.417: And many shall be utterly destroyed
14.418: And others kill each other by hard fights.
14.419: And then one covered with dark scales shall come;
14.420: Two others shall come acting in concert
14.421: With one another, and with them a third
14.422: A great ram from Cyrene, whom before.
14.423: I spoke of as a fugitive in war
14.424: Beside the streams of Nile; but in no wise
14.425: An unsuccessful way do all complete.
14.426: And then the lengths of the revolving years
14.427: Shall be exceeding quiet; yet again
14.428: Thereafter shall a second war for them
14.429: In Egypt be stirred up, and there shall be
14.430: A battle on the sea, but victory
14.431: Shall not be theirs. Ah, wretched ones, there shall
14.432: A conquest of the famous city be,
14.433: And it shall be a spoil of war not long.
14.434: And then men having common boundaries
14.435: Of much land shall flee wretched, and shall lead
14.436: Their wretched parents. And they shall again
14.437: Having great victory light on a land,
14.438: And shall destroy the Jews, men staunch in war,
14.439: Wasting by wars far as the hoary deep,
14.440: On both sides, fighting in the foremost ranks
14.441: For father-land and parents. And a race
14.442: Of trophy-bearing men shall for the dead
14.443: Be reckoned. Ah, how many men shall swim
14.444: About the waves! For on the sandy beach
14.445: Many shall lie; and heads of golden hair
14.446: Shall fall beneath Egyptian winged fowls.
14.447: And then for the Arabians mortal blood
14.448: Shall go in quest. But when wolves shall with dogs
14.449: Pledge in a sea-girt island solemn oaths,
14.450: Then shall there be the raising of a tower,
14.451: And the city that suffered very many things
14.452: Men shall inhabit. For deceitful gold
14.453: Shall no more be nor silver, nor acquiring
14.454: Of the earth, nor much-laboring servitude;
14.455: But one fast friendship and one mode of life
14.456: With cheerful soul; and all things shall be common
14.457: And equal light among the means of life.
14.458: And wickedness shall sink down from the earth
14.459: Into the vast sea. And then near at hand
14.460: Is come the harvest-time of mortal men.
14.461: There is imposed a strong necessity
14.462: That these things be fulfilled. And at that time
14.463: There shall not any other traveler say,
14.464: In this conjecturing, that the race of men
14.465: Though perishable shall ever cease to be.
14.466: And then a holy nation shall prevail
14.467: And bold the sovereignty of all the earth
14.468: Unto all ages with their mighty sons.
9.1: (There is no Book 9 of the Sibylline Oracles: the manuscript tradition numbers the collection 1–8 and 11–14.)
10.1: (There is no Book 10 of the Sibylline Oracles: the manuscript tradition numbers the collection 1–8 and 11–14.)
